Introducing

The Curious
Collection

Giant questions in small stories,
for curious minds of any age.

Discover the Books

Why these books exist

Children are born philosophers.
We just forget to tell them.

∿ ∿ ∿

Some of the most important questions a person will ever wrestle with are the ones they first encounter before they can even tie their shoes. What makes something itself? Why do words mean what they mean? Is it better to be young or old — and what does each know that the other doesn't?

These are not questions reserved for graduate seminars or late-night conversations. They belong to everyone. They always have. And the best time to introduce them is when a mind is still wide open — before we learn to feel embarrassed by wonder, before curiosity gets crowded out by certainty.

"I won an award in first grade — for asking questions. I never stopped."

The Curious Collection was born from a simple conviction: that picture books can carry real philosophical weight without losing an ounce of their warmth, humor, or delight. That a boy and his boat can quietly introduce the Ship of Theseus. That a bear who wonders why he isn't called a FLUFFLEBOOTS can crack open the whole mystery of language.

These books don't tell children what to think. They create the conditions for thinking — and then leave the question deliciously open. Each story is written to work on multiple levels at once: a read-aloud for a four-year-old, a conversation-starter for a classroom, a quiet gut-punch for the parent reading it for the tenth time.

Giant questions in small stories, for curious minds of any age.

The Collection

Two books. Infinite questions.

∿ ∿ ∿
Ship of Theseus for little ones

Billy's Bothersome Boat

Ages 4–18  ·  and the adults who love them

Billy has a boat he loves. The boat is old. Plank by plank, Billy saves his lunch money and replaces every rotting board — until the day he looks at his beautiful new boat and wonders: is this still the same boat? And when he rebuilds the old planks into a second vessel, which one is his?

A tender, funny story about love, identity, and what it means for something to remain itself through change — that quietly introduces one of philosophy's oldest thought experiments to anyone patient enough to sit with a good question.

Explore Billy's story →
"Which one of you is my boat?"
Philosophy of language, age 3+

Fluffleboots

Ages 3–103  ·  and everyone in between

Why aren't dogs called FLUFFLEBOOTS? Why is blue called blue at all? Someone made these words up once — and everyone just agreed. Nobody asked the elephant what to call her nose. Nobody asked the bumblebee. Nobody asked the knee.

A riotously funny, deeply subversive book about language, naming, and the wild possibility that you could name things too — that invites children to understand, from the very first page, that words are invented, not discovered. And that maybe some of them deserve to be reinvented.

Explore Fluffleboots →
"So what's YOUR word for thunder? Shout it loud and far!"

About the author

Melissa Korzun

Melissa Korzun has spent her life asking the questions other people forgot to ask. It started in first grade — where she won an award for it — and it never stopped.

The Curious Collection grew from a deeply held belief: that curiosity is not a childhood phase to be outgrown, but a way of moving through the world that should be nurtured from the very first story a child hears. That the best questions don't have clean answers. That wonder is a practice, not a phase.

These books are an invitation — to children to think out loud, to parents to sit with uncertainty alongside their kids, and to teachers to let a question breathe in a room before rushing to fill the silence.

Melissa is currently seeking a publisher and illustrator to bring Billy's Bothersome Boat and Fluffleboots — and the rest of the Collection — fully to life.
